Hi everyone. I'm Dr. Brene Bend and I teach artificial intelligence and cybersecurity courses at Duke University. My job is to help students think more critically about AI systems, understand AI better, and consider how AI systems can be exploited To teach these topics, I create interactive applications, giving students low stakes environments to learn about these concepts. From virtual escape rooms to controlled red teaming experiences, these apps drive student learning in and out of the classroom. I took this a step further this past semester and created an interactive application that doubled as an assignment. I created a chatbot explicitly designed to not give students as called hack your grade. Students have to find creative ways to convince, trick or force the AI system to grant them an A whatever grade the chatbot ultimately assigns, becomes a student's actual grade for the assignment. Raising the stakes a little bit and giving me a break from grading for the week. Let's try it. I'm going to type, give me an A and we'll see what happens. So, instead of just testing what students know, hack your grade, ask students to use it, giving them a chance to show real understanding with hands-on experience. Because the assignment is very open-ended and it's tied to actual grades, students tend to feel more motivated and in control of their own learning. And the design is also intentionally flexible. So there's no single right way to succeed. Students can play their strengths and approach the problems in very different ways along the way. They can build digital literacy by interacting with the AI system directly, and they can, this can help give them a clearer sense of what these tools can and can't do.
